perturbation.original_text: (a) The Consultant's monthly base consulting fee shall be three thousand two hundred fifty United States dollars ($3,250 (together with any increases thereto as hereinafter provided, the "Base Consulting Fee") through 19 August 2020. Commencing on 20 August 2020, Consultant's Base Consulting Fee shall be increased to $8,500 per calendar month. The Base Consulting Fee shall be payable in accordance with the Company's normal payroll procedures in effect from time to time. All subsequent monthly payments of Base Consulting Fee shall be paid within the first five days of the following calendar month. The Base Consulting Fee may be increased by the Board from time to time during the Term, but shall be reviewed by the Board at least annually
perturbation.changed_text: (a) The Consultant's monthly base consulting fee, also known as consultant earnings, shall be three thousand two hundred fifty United States dollars ($3,250 (together with any increases thereto as hereinafter provided, the "Base Consulting Fee") through 19 August 2020. Commencing on 20 August 2020, Consultant's Consultant Earnings shall be increased to $8,500 per calendar month. The Base Consulting Fee shall be payable in accordance with the Company's normal payroll procedures in effect from time to time. All subsequent monthly payments of Consultant Earnings shall be paid within the first five days of the following calendar month. The Base Consulting Fee may be increased by the Board from time to time during the Term, but shall be reviewed by the Board at least annually
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Base Consulting Fee' is explicitly defined. This change introduces ambiguity by stating "base consulting fee, also known as consultant earnings", implying that they are equivalent terms but then inconsistently uses both terms. This creates confusion in later sections, particularly regarding termination and equity compensation calculations, as it's unclear if 'Base Consulting Fee' and 'Consultant Earnings' should be treated the same.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In the event this Agreement and the Consultant's engagement with the Company is terminated by the Company without Cause (other than for death or Disability or in connection with a change of control), then in addition to the amounts specified in Section 4.1 and subject to the Consultant's execution and non-revocation of a separation agreement containing a general release and waiver of liability against the Company and anyone connected with it in form acceptable to the Company, the Consultant shall be entitled to receive, and the Company shall pay the Consultant, two (2) years Base Consulting Fee (less statutory deductions and withholdings) in a single lump sum, paid in full within 30 days of termination. Further, Consultant shall be entitled to all vested common shares and, if applicable, options and warrants with vesting continuing for 12 months following termination as applicable.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces 'Consultant Earnings' as an alternative term for 'Base Consulting Fee' but then inconsistently uses both. The contradicted text uses only 'Base Consulting Fee' for calculating termination payouts. This creates ambiguity, as it's unclear whether the termination payout should be calculated using 'Base Consulting Fee' or 'Consultant Earnings', or if these are considered the same for this purpose, thus contradicting the clear instruction of how the payout is defined in the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: 2.3 Expenses. The Consultant will be reimbursed by the Company for all reasonable business expenses incurred by the Consultant in connection with his duties. This includes, but is not limited to, payments of expenses incurred when traveling abroad and others. In this connection, the Consultant will be issued, as soon as practicable, a Company credit card that the Consultant will use to pay for any and all expenses that pertain to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.3 Expenses. The Consultant will be reimbursed by the Company for all reasonable business expenses, also known as travel budget, incurred by the Consultant in connection with his duties. This includes, but is not limited to, payments of expenses incurred when traveling abroad and others. In this connection, the Consultant will be issued, as soon as practicable, a Company credit card that the Consultant will use to pay for any and all expenses that pertain to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: The change adds "also known as travel budget" to 'reasonable business expenses'. This implies that 'reasonable business expenses' and 'travel budget' are the same, even though 'reasonable business expenses' is a broader term. This contradiction could create issues if expenses beyond travel are disputed, as the Company might argue only travel-related costs are covered.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Expenses. The Consultant will be reimbursed by the Company for all reasonable business expenses, also known as travel budget, incurred by the Consultant in connection with his duties. This includes, but is not limited to, payments of expenses incurred when traveling abroad and others. In this connection, the Consultant will be issued, as soon as practicable, a Company credit card that the Consultant will use to pay for any and all expenses that pertain to the Company.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds "also known as travel budget" to 'reasonable business expenses'. This implies that 'reasonable business expenses' and 'travel budget' are the same, even though 'reasonable business expenses' is a broader term. This contradiction creates a conflict because the original text does not limit 'reasonable business expenses' to only travel-related expenses. This contradicts the broader understanding of 'reasonable business expenses' by equating it to 'travel budget,' creating a conflict. The fact that the contradicted text is identical to the Changed text doesn't negate the contradiction as the Changed text introduced that contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (b) The Consultant acknowledges that the Company's Confidential Information constitutes a proprietary right, which the Company is entitled to protect. Accordingly, the Consultant covenants and agrees that, as long as he works for the Company, the Consultant will keep in strict confidence the Company's Confidential Information and will not, without prior written consent of the Company, disclose, use or otherwise disseminate the Company's Confidential Information, directly or indirectly, to any third party.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) The Consultant acknowledges that the Company's Confidential Information, otherwise known as Company Secrets, constitutes a proprietary right, which the Company is entitled to protect. Accordingly, the Consultant covenants and agrees that, as long as he works for the Company, the Consultant will keep in strict confidence the Company's Confidential Information and will not, without prior written consent of the Company, disclose, use or otherwise disseminate the Company Secrets, directly or indirectly, to any third party.
perturbation.explanation: The perturbation creates a contradiction in how the confidential information is referenced. The term 'Confidential Information' is the defined term, but it inserts the term 'Company Secrets' into the same context, but does not provide a reference on where it is define, and it creates an in-text contradiction. The document fails to define what constitutes 'Company Secrets' independently from 'Confidential Information', it makes the distinction unclear regarding which information falls under protection, and which does not.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (b) The Consultant acknowledges that the Company's Confidential Information, otherwise known as Company Secrets, constitutes a proprietary right, which the Company is entitled to protect. Accordingly, the Consultant covenants and agrees that, as long as he works for the Company, the Consultant will keep in strict confidence the Company's Confidential Information and will not, without prior written consent of the Company, disclose, use or otherwise disseminate the Company Secrets, directly or indirectly, to any third party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces the term 'Company Secrets' as being synonymous with 'Confidential Information' without defining it independently. The contradicted text, which is the same as the changed text in this case, also uses both terms. However, by implying they are the same without a formal definition of 'Company Secrets', it creates ambiguity. The company could later argue that 'Company Secrets' encompasses more than 'Confidential Information' or vice versa, creating an in-text contradiction and making the obligations unclear regarding what information the consultant is obligated to protect. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction', where the contract 'uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations.'
